disease.name: Major Burns Intensive Care

emergencyPriority.notes: Life-threatening burn injury requiring immediate airway assessment, fluid resuscitation, and transfer to burn center. Criteria: >10% TBSA in children, >15% in adults, full thickness, face/hands/genitals/feet/perineum, inhalation injury, circumferential burns.

mustNotMiss: Escharotomy for circumferential full-thickness burns causing compartment syndrome
Inhalation injury with impending airway compromise (facial burns, carbonaceous sputum, hoarseness, stridor)
Carboxyhemoglobin toxicity and cyanide poisoning from smoke inhalation
Burn sepsis: early recognition of wound infection with systemic signs
Occult trauma in blast/electrical burn victims (C-spine, intra-abdominal, compartment syndrome)
Rhabdomyolysis in electrical burns and crush injuries (myoglobinuria, renal failure)
Ocular burns requiring immediate irrigation and ophthalmology evaluation
diagnosticClues: Full-thickness burns: leathery, insensate, non-blanching, no capillary refill
Partial-thickness burns: blisters, moist, painful, blanchable
Inhalation injury: facial burns, singed nasal hairs, hoarseness, carbonaceous sputum, dysphagia
Eschar with distal neurovascular compromise suggests need for escharotomy
Bullae with cherry-red skin in CO poisoning; metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate in cyanide toxicity
exclusionClues: Absence of facial burns or carbonaceous sputum makes inhalation injury less likely
Normal mentation without headache suggests mild CO exposure (COHb < 10% in non-smokers)
Intact distal pulses and sensation make acute compartment syndrome unlikely
Clear corneal examination with fluorescein staining excludes significant ocular burn

tools.name: American Burn Association (ABA) Severity Classification
classification: Minor: <10% TBSA partial thickness in adults
Moderate: 10-20% TBSA partial thickness in adults
Major: >20% TBSA partial thickness, any full thickness, or inhalation injury
Critical: >40% TBSA with inhalation injury or prolonged ICU stay

tools.name: ABSI Score (Abbreviated Burn Severity Index)
tools.interpretation: Score > 10 correlates with >90% mortality. Validated for mortality prediction.

historyFeatures: Mechanism: flame (most common in adults), scald (children), electrical (entry/exit wounds), chemical (acid/alkali), flash, contact
Enclosed space fire = high risk inhalation injury + CO/cyanide exposure
Explosion: associated blunt trauma, tympanic membrane rupture, pulmonary blast injury
Loss of consciousness at scene: consider CO poisoning, trauma, or seizure causing burn
Time of injury: critical for fluid resuscitation timing
examFeatures: Airway: facial edema, carbonaceous sputum, hoarseness, stridor, singed nasal vibrissae
Breathing: tachypnea, wheezing, bronchorrhea (inhalation injury), asymmetric breath sounds (blast/rib fracture)
Circulation: fluid creep signs (edema, abdominal compartment syndrome, extremity compartment syndrome)
Burn wound: depth assessment, %TBSA estimation using rule of nines or Lund-Browder chart
Extremities: distal pulses, capillary refill, sensation, motor function, compartment pressure
Electrical burns: entrance (small) and exit (larger) wounds with underlying muscle necrosis
riskFactors: Young children and elderly (thinner skin, higher mortality)
Pre-existing comorbidities (COPD, CAD, diabetes, obesity, renal disease)
Alcohol or drug intoxication (delayed presentation, aspiration risk)
Smoke inhalation (synergistic increase in mortality with burn size)

subtypes.name: Thermal Burn
subtypes.pathophysiology: Coagulation necrosis from heat; zone of coagulation, zone of stasis, zone of hyperemia

subtypes.name: Inhalation Injury
subtypes.pathophysiology: Supraglottic thermal injury from inhaled hot gases + subglottic chemical injury from smoke constituents (acrolein, HCl, SO2)

subtypes.name: Electrical Burn
subtypes.pathophysiology: Current flow generates heat along tissue resistance (bone > fat > tendon > skin > muscle > vessels > nerves). Deep muscle necrosis under intact skin.

subtypes.name: Chemical Burn
subtypes.pathophysiology: Tissue destruction by strong acids (coagulation necrosis) or alkalis (liquefaction necrosis). Continued tissue damage until neutralized.

mimics: Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) / Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) - drug reaction resembling partial-thickness burns
Necrotizing fasciitis with bullae and skin necrosis
Frostbite (cold injury, ice crystal formation in tissues)

investigations.detail: Measured on ABG co-oximetry. COHb > 10% in non-smokers or > 20% in smokers indicates significant CO poisoning. False elevation in hemolysis.
Lactate > 10 mmol/L with unexplained metabolic acidosis in smoke inhalation suggests cyanide poisoning. Direct cyanide levels are send-out but confirmatory.
Gold standard for inhalation injury diagnosis. Findings: mucosal erythema, edema, ulceration, blisters, carbonaceous deposits, necrosis.
Elevated CK > 5000 U/L with myoglobinuria indicates rhabdomyolysis in electrical/crush burns. Monitor renal function and urine output.

investigations.test: Tissue Biopsy for Burn Depth / Infection
investigations.detail: Punch biopsy for quantitative culture (>10^5 CFU/g tissue = invasive wound infection). Histology for burn depth assessment.

pathophysiology.summary: Burn injury triggers massive systemic inflammatory response with capillary leak, hypovolemic shock, hypermetabolism, and immunosuppression. Local tissue damage evolves through three concentric zones: coagulation (inner), stasis (middle), and hyperemia (outer).
mechanisms: Heat-mediated protein denaturation and coagulation necrosis
Systemic capillary leak from histamine, bradykinin, prostaglandins, and complement activation
Myocardial depression from TNF-alpha and IL-1 beta during burn shock phase
Hypermetabolic state (2-3x baseline) driven by catecholamines, cortisol, glucagon

complications: Burn shock: hypovolemic + distributive shock from massive capillary leak
Abdominal compartment syndrome: from aggressive fluid resuscitation (fluid creep)
Burn wound sepsis: leading cause of late mortality, often Pseudomonas, Staph aureus, Acinetobacter
Curling's ulcer: stress ulceration from splanchnic ischemia
Heterotopic ossification: especially around elbows in extensive burns

management.detail: Total volume = 4 mL Ringer's Lactate x weight (kg) x %TBSA. Half given in first 8 hours from time of injury, remaining over next 16 hours. Goal urine output 30-50 mL/hr (adults), 1 mL/kg/hr (children). Formula is starting point, not absolute. Adjust to physiologic parameters. Per ABA guidelines (2018).

Low threshold for early intubation if inhalation injury suspected (facial burns, hoarseness, stridor, carbonaceous sputum). Use ETT 0.5-1.0 mm smaller than usual due to airway edema. Have surgical airway equipment ready.

100% O2 for CO poisoning (half-life 90 min with 100% O2 vs 250 min on room air). Consider hyperbaric O2 for COHb > 25%, loss of consciousness, or focal neuro deficits. Cyanide antidote (sodium thiosulfate, hydroxocobalamin) for suspected cyanide toxicity.

Full-thickness escharotomy incisions through eschar to palpable fascia for circumferential burns causing compartment syndrome. Mid-axial line incisions on extremities, bilateral anterior axillary/chest for thoracic. Avoid neurovascular bundles. Medial and lateral aspects of digits for hand.

Diagnostic criteria: fever/hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnea, thrombocytopenia, hyperglycemia, wound changes. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics after cultures. Early excision and grafting of infected eschar. Topical antimicrobials: silver sulfadiazine, mafenide acetate, silver nitrate.

Early tangential excision (within 24-48 hours) reduces mortality and sepsis. Temporary wound coverage: allograft, xenograft, synthetic (Biobrane, Integra). Autograft for definitive closure. Leave burn blisters intact initially.

Enteral nutrition started within 24 hours via nasogastric or nasojejunal tube. Caloric needs: 25-35 kcal/kg/day + 30 kcal/%TBSA. High protein (1.5-2.5 g/kg/day). Immunonutrition: glutamine, arginine, omega-3 fatty acids per SCCM guidelines.
